best_candidate.title: Dynamic density functional theory of multicomponent cellular membranes

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://arxiv.org/abs/2112.08651, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

best_candidate.abstract: We present a continuum model trained on molecular dynamics (MD) simulations for cellular membranes composed of an arbitrary number of lipid types. The model is constructed within the formalism of dynamic density functional theory and can be extended to include features such as the presence of proteins and membrane deformations. This framework represents a paradigm shift by enabling simulations that can access length scales on the order of microns and time scales on the order of seconds, all while maintaining near fidelity to the underlying MD models. These length and time scales are significant for accessing biological processes associated with signaling pathways within cells. Membrane interactions with RAS, a protein implicated in roughly 30% of human cancers, are considered as an application. Simulation results are presented and verified with MD simulations, and implications of this new capability are discussed.

data.title: Effect of cholesterol on the ion-membrane interaction: Zeta potential and dynamic light scattering study.

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://api.unpaywall.org/v2/10.1016/j.chemphyslip.2023.105307?email=<INSERT_YOUR_EMAIL> or https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chemphyslip.2023.105307, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

data.abstract: Cholesterol in a bio-membrane plays a significant role in many cellular event and is known to regulate the functional activity of protein and ion channel. In this study we report a significant effect of cholesterol on the ion-membrane interaction. We prepare large unilamellar vesicles, composed of zwitterionic lipid DOPC and anionic lipid DOPG with different cholesterol concentration. Electrostatics of anionic membranes containing cholesterol in the presence of NaCl has systematically been explored using dynamic light scattering and zeta potential. Negative zeta potential of the membrane decreases its negative value with increasing ion concentration for all cholesterol concentrations. However, zeta potential itself decreases with increasing cholesterol content even in the absence of monovalent ions. Electrostatic behaviour of the membrane is determined from well-known Gouy Chapmann model. Negative surface charge density of the membrane decreases with increasing cholesterol content. Binding constant, estimated from the electrostatic double layer theory, is found to increase significantly in the presence of cholesterol. Comparison of electrostatic parameters of the membrane in the presence and absence of cholesterol suggests that cholesterol significantly alter the electrostatic behaviour of the membrane.

data.title: Anisotropic interactions for continuum modeling of protein-membrane systems.

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://api.unpaywall.org/v2/10.1063/5.0237408?email=<INSERT_YOUR_EMAIL> or https://doi.org/10.1063/5.0237408, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

data.abstract: In this work, a model for anisotropic interactions between proteins and cellular membranes is proposed for large-scale continuum simulations. The framework of the model is based on dynamic density functional theory, which provides a formalism to describe the lipid densities within the membrane as continuum fields while still maintaining the fidelity of the underlying molecular interactions. Within this framework, we extend recent results to include the anisotropic effects of protein-lipid interactions. As applications, we consider two membrane proteins of biological interest: a RAS-RAF complex tethered to the membrane and a membrane embedded G protein-coupled receptor. A strong qualitative and quantitative agreement is found between the numerical results and the corresponding molecular dynamics simulations. Combining the scope of continuum level simulations with the details from molecular level particle simulations enables research into protein-membrane behaviors at a more biologically relevant scale, which crucially can also be accessed via experiment.

journal.name: Journal of Applied Membrane Science & Technology

data.abstract:
This book [1] provides a comprehensive overview of the roles of membrane technology in the petroleum industry, covering membrane selection, materials, challenges, and applications across the upstream sector in oil and gas production and midstream refining. It includes applications for enhanced oil recovery, midstream refining processes for hydrocarbon separation, and contaminant capture, especially for environmental remediation, focusing on the treatment of byproducts like hydrogen sulfide from petroleum processing. It encompasses three main areas: a general conceptualization of membrane technology, core topics on membrane applications in various sectors within the petroleum industry, and future perspectives along with modeling.
The first two chapters introduce membrane development, explaining how membranes work, their classifications, and the current challenges of membrane technology, including fouling and solute transport modeling. Chapter 2 provides insights into novel membrane materials, categorized as synthetic polymers, biopolymers, and inorganic materials, as well as the use of nanomaterials to nanocomposite membrane for enhancing membrane performance. The following chapters explore membrane applications across the petroleum industry, discussing the importance and challenges in separating compounds with similar physical properties, such as the selective separation of gas mixtures containing carbon dioxide (CO₂), hydrogen sulphide (H₂S), and liquefied petroleum gas (LPG)-range hydrocarbons. For instance, Chapter 3 on natural gas processing recommends different types of membranes depending on the target gas to be removed, focusing on enhancing gas transport selectivity and permeability.
Subsequent chapters delve into complex hydrocarbon separations, which traditionally require energy-intensive cryogenic distillation but can be addressed with membrane technology. Chapter 4 details the selective separation of olefins from paraffins produced after catalytic cracking, using various membrane types, such as polymeric, supported liquid, carbon molecular sieve, and mixed matrix membranes. The importance of separating olefins, vital for the petrochemical industry, is highlighted. Chapter 5 discusses the separation of benzene-toluene-xylene (BTX) from aliphatic paraffins in naphtha, emphasizing the solution-diffusion mechanism via selective aromatic absorption and noting that nanocomposite membranes mitigate the swelling issues common in polymeric membranes. Chapter 11 shifts to upstream processes, detailing how nanofiltration can produce ionically modified water with controlled hardness and reduced sulfate, useful for enhanced oil recovery (EOR) in creating smart water for flooding applications.
The book then addresses the capture of pollutants produced in the petroleum industry. Produced water, a wastewater stream containing salts, minerals, and emulsified oils generated during oil and gas extraction, presents persistent environmental challenges if untreated. Chapter 6 provides a summary of produced water characteristics, including compound composition and wastewater metrics like total dissolved solids (TDS), chemical oxygen demand (COD), biochemical oxygen demand (BOD), and gases. Chapter 7 reviews conventional treatment techniques, highlighting the need for hybrid processes to handle its complex composition. Chapter 8 details how membrane properties like pore size, surface wettability, and roughness affect oil-water selectivity, which can be tuned by modifying membrane hydrophilicity, resulting in either superhydrophilic or superoleophilic membranes. In Chapters 9 and 10, phenolic and heavy metal removal from refining wastewater is explored, detailing size exclusion and adsorption mechanisms, with innovative methods like pervaporation and photocatalytic membranes for the removal of phenolic compounds. Heavy metals can be removed using multiple types of membranes, including ultrafiltration, nanofiltration, reverse osmosis, micellar-enhanced ultrafiltration, and forward osmosis. Chapter 12 highlights the role of pervaporation membranes in removing organosulfur compounds from gasoline, noting polyimides as a common membrane material, with alternatives such as polydimethylsiloxane and ceramics. The book also emphasizes simulation tools that enable molecular and fluid dynamic predictions, including molecular dynamics, density functional theory, Monte Carlo simulations, and computational fluid dynamics. The final chapter briefly discusses fouling mitigation, membrane reusability, and greener fabrication approaches.
Overall, this book serves as an extensive resource on membrane technologies across various sectors of the petroleum industry, including enhanced oil recovery (EOR), selective hydrocarbon separation, gas sweetening, and waste stream treatment. It provides a solid foundation in material science, separation mechanisms, and solute transport modeling. The book covers a wide range of current research and briefly highlights potential applications of membrane technology in the petroleum industry. For instance, it mentions emerging applications such as supported liquid membranes (SLM) for olefin-paraffin separation in Chapter 4 and Liqui-Cel membrane contactors for polyphenol extraction in Chapter 9. However, challenges like the trade-off between permeability and selectivity, fouling, and the declining mechanical stability of membranes during extended operations remain barriers to broader implementation of membrane technology in the petroleum industry. The book addresses issues such as the physical aging of polymeric membranes in separating multicomponent gas mixtures, which leads to reduced selectivity over time.
The book could benefit from greater emphasis on commercially available membranes and prototype systems, with a focus on scalability, durability, and performance, which are the key considerations for industry adoption. Additionally, it would be valuable to include more recent updates on the adaptation of membrane technology in the industry. For example, Honeywell UOP’s Separex™ system is used for natural gas sweetening, removing contaminants such as water, carbon dioxide, and hydrogen sulfide, as highlighted on their official website. Air Products Membrane Solutions' PRISM membrane separators, featuring polymeric hollow fibers, are utilized in offshore drilling to separate gases like methane, nitrogen, or hydrogen. Similarly, MTR Corporation’s VaporSep® system has been successfully employed for olefin-paraffin separation, enhancing hydrocarbon recovery. These are just a few examples of successful commercial applications of membrane technology, though a comprehensive list is beyond the scope of this review. Notably, the book lacks sufficient coverage of hydrogen recovery from hydroprocessing using membrane technology, an area of significant industrial interest. Including detailed techno-economic feasibility studies would further enhance the book's practical relevance, fostering wider adoption of membrane technologies in petroleum processes.

data.title: Natural payload delivery of the doxorubicin anticancer drug from boron nitride oxide nanosheets

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://api.unpaywall.org/v2/10.1016/J.APSUSC.2018.12.273?email=<INSERT_YOUR_EMAIL> or https://doi.org/10.1016/J.APSUSC.2018.12.273, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

data.abstract: Abstract We studied the behavior of doxorubicin (DOX; an anticancer drug) molecules loaded on a boron nitride oxide nanosheet (BNO-NS) using the density functional theory (DFT), time-dependent density functional theory (TDDFT), and molecular dynamic (MD) simulation methods. We found that DOX molecules in π-π or covalent interaction with BNO-NS preserve their optical properties in water. Moreover, the BNO-NS vector allowed stabilizing the DOX molecules on a cellular membrane contrary to isolated DOX that randomly moved in the solvent box without any interaction with the cell membrane. From these results, we conclude that hydrophilic BNO-NS represents a good candidate for DOX molecule transport and stabilization near a cell membrane. In this drug delivery system, the choice of BNO-NS as nanovector is important because it allows delivering an elevated therapeutic dose directly on the cancer cell target without hindrance of the DOX payload.

journal.name: The journal of physical chemistry. B

data.abstract: Voltage-sensitive dyes (VSDs) are fluorescent molecules that detect changes in the membrane potential, making them invaluable for studying electrical activity in neurons, cardiac cells, and other excitable tissues. They are widely used in neuroscience and physiology to visualize and measure real-time voltage dynamics in cellular networks and whole tissues. VSDs exhibit a high sensitivity to their surrounding environment, which leads to notable solvation relaxation and a substantial Stokes shift upon excitation. Accurate prediction of their fluorescence spectra requires an advanced solvation model that captures these dynamic effects. In this work, we extend the Similarity Transformed Equation-of-Motion Domain-Based Local Pair Natural Orbital Coupled Cluster with Singles and Doubles (STEOM-DLPNO-CCSD) method, a computationally efficient approach for vertical excitation energies, to predict fluorescence spectra for VSDs. While the default perturbative solvation correction at the Hartree-Fock level has proven effective for some excited state calculations, it fails to account for the electron correlation effects that are crucial for accurate fluorescence spectra predictions. To address this, we incorporate a time-dependent density functional theory-based perturbative solvation correction, which improves the accuracy of the methods by better capturing the necessary correlation effects. The methodology is validated through studies of two carefully selected VSDs, (E)-3-(4-(2-(6-(dibutylamino)naphthalen-2-yl)vinyl)pyridin-1-ium-1-yl)propane-1-sulfonate (di-4-ANEPPS) and 4-((E)-4-((E)-4-(diethylamino)-2-methoxystyryl)styryl)-2-(6-(dimethylamino)-3-(dimethyliminio)-3H-xanthen-9-yl)benzenesulfonate (sRhoVR-1). The developed quantum chemical protocol allows for accurate prediction of fluorescence maxima for dyes with a predominant excited state and can accommodate computational constraints without greatly compromising precision. However, the study also highlights the need for further improvements for the prediction of peak intensity, suggesting that explicit solvent models or hybrid quantum mechanics/molecular mechanics (QM/MM) approaches could be valuable for future work. The proposed method provides a powerful tool for the design of VSDs optimized for specific environments and applications.

data.title: Elucidation of the molecular interactions that enable stable assembly and structural diversity in multicomponent immune receptors
data.venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://api.unpaywall.org/v2/10.1101/2020.12.22.424036?email=<INSERT_YOUR_EMAIL> or https://doi.org/10.1101/2020.12.22.424036, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

journal.name: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences

data.abstract: Significance Receptors that separate ligand recognition and intracellular signaling into separate protein subunits are ubiquitous in immunity. These subunits mix and match to create combinatorial functional diversity. The transmembrane domains of these receptors assemble through the interaction between two acidic and one basic residue on different helices. Using computational methods to study the DAP12-NKG2C receptor complex, we identified a polar motif in which a singly-protonated Asp-Asp pair forms a carboxyl-carboxylate clamp that clasps a charged Lys side chain in the membrane. This local interaction allows dynamic variations in other regions of the helices that tolerate sequence diversity of the interacting subunits in this class of receptors, which signal through multisubunit clustering rather than propagation of rigid conformational changes through the membrane. Multicomponent immune receptors are essential complexes in which distinct ligand-recognition and signaling subunits are held together by interactions between acidic and basic residues of their transmembrane helices. A 2:1 acidic-to-basic motif in the transmembrane domains of the subunits is necessary and sufficient to assemble these receptor complexes. Here, we study a prototype for these receptors, a DAP12-NKG2C 2:1 heterotrimeric complex, in which the two DAP12 subunits each contribute a single transmembrane Asp residue, and the NKG2C subunit contributes a Lys to form the complex. DAP12 can also associate with 20 other subunits using a similar motif. Here, we use molecular-dynamics simulations to understand the basis for the high affinity and diversity of interactions in this group of receptors. Simulations of the transmembrane helices with differing protonation states of the Asp-Asp-Lys triad identified a structurally stable interaction in which a singly-protonated Asp-Asp pair forms a hydrogen-bonded carboxyl-carboxylate clamp that clasps onto a charged Lys side chain. This polar motif was also supported by density functional theory and a Protein Data Bank–wide search. In contrast, the helices are dynamic at sites distal to the stable carboxyl-carboxylate clamp motif. Such a locally stable but globally dynamic structure is well suited to accommodate the sequence and structural variations in the transmembrane helices of multicomponent receptors, which mix and match subunits to create combinatorial functional diversity from a limited number of subunits. It also supports a signaling mechanism based on multisubunit clustering rather than propagation of rigid conformational changes through the membrane.

title: Dynamic density functional theory of multicomponent cellular membranes
